title: OS commands should not be vulnerable to command injection attacks
description: OS command injections occur when applications build command lines from untrusted data before executing them with a system shell. In that case, an attacker can tamper with the command line construction and force the execution of unexpected commands. This can lead to the compromise of the underlying operating system.
recommendation: Review and fix according to the rule guidelines.

noncompliant:
string fileName = Request.Form["file"];
Process.Start("cmd", $"/c del {fileName}"); // Noncompliant: command injection possible
compliant:
string fileName = Path.GetFileName(Request.Form["file"]);
File.Delete(fileName); // Compliant: use File.Delete instead of shell command

checks.message: Process.Start with shell command and dynamic content - potential command injection vulnerability

logic.detection_conditions: Uses regex to detect Process.Start calls to shell commands (cmd, powershell, etc.) with dynamic content
logic.triggering_conditions: CallExpression nodes with Process.Start using shell commands combined with string interpolation or concatenation
logic.exclusion_conditions: Excludes test methods, logging, ProcessStartInfo usage, and calls with sanitization methods
logic.reasoning: Detects Process.Start calls to shell commands (cmd, powershell, bash, sh) that use dynamic content via string interpolation or concatenation. These patterns are vulnerable to command injection attacks.

should_not_trigger: ProcessStartInfo psi = new ProcessStartInfo("ping", safeInput); Process.Start(psi);
[Test] public void TestCommand() { Process.Start("cmd", "/c ping " + input); }
